message:
fix(analytics): normalize epoch units in drift detection to handle both seconds and milliseconds

The query_status() drift detection was comparing last_updated timestamps
using seconds-based epoch math, but some database rows store timestamps
in milliseconds. This caused fresh tracks to appear stale (a millisecond
timestamp like 1700000000000 compared against a seconds-based threshold
always appears astronomically old).

Changes:
- Added normalize_epoch_millis() helper that detects second-based values
  (0..100_000_000_000 range, covers years ~1970-5138) and converts them
  to milliseconds via saturating_mul(1000), leaving millisecond values
  unchanged and negative values passthrough
- Added is_recently_updated() helper that normalizes before comparing
  against the threshold, replacing duplicated inline freshness checks
- Changed now_epoch computation from as_secs() to as_millis() and
  stale_threshold from 86400 seconds to 86_400_000 milliseconds
- Updated track_a_fresh and track_b_fresh to use is_recently_updated()

Tests added:
- normalize_epoch_millis_preserves_negative_millisecond_values: negative
  and second-range values handled correctly
- query_status_treats_millisecond_timestamps_as_fresh: both tracks with
  recent millisecond timestamps show as fresh with "none" action
- query_status_supports_legacy_second_timestamps: both tracks with recent
  second timestamps are auto-normalized and show as fresh
- query_status_detects_millisecond_freshness_mismatch: stale track B
  triggers "rebuild_track_b" with track_freshness_mismatch signal

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
